name: Adam Clayton Powell, Jr.
abstract: Adam Clayton Powell Jr. (November 29, 1908 – April 4, 1972) was an American Baptist pastor and politician who represented the Harlem neighborhood of New York City in the United States House of Representatives from 1945 until 1971.

concerts.label: Negro Gospel Music Festival featuring Mahalia Jackson
Gospel and Religious Music Festival featuring Mahalia Jackson
Negro Gospel and Religious Music Festival featuring Mahalia Jackson
Testimonial Meeting: Salute to A. Philip Randolph

about_quotes.speaker: Luther Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: there was an Adam Powell Sr. (chuckles), contrary to popular opinion, Adam Powell Jr. just didn't, didn't come on the scene, and my mother was very much involved in that area.
about_quotes.doc_title: Luther Henderson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Adam Powell was leaving the -- he was the first Negro councilman in New York and he was leaving the council to go to Congress... And he threw his support behind Ben Davis

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: Adam Clayton Powell was writing a column... really was trying to stir up people into speaking up for themselves... Adam was a brilliant man and a real social activist.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Luther Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER mentions 'Adam Powell's wife' and the interviewee discusses 'Adam's rib' in the context of interpreting the title 'A Drum is a Woman' by Adam Powell.
about_quotes.doc_title: Luther Henderson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Andrew Blakeney
about_quotes.evidence: Who was that that once said--I think Adam Clayton Powell, or someone, said there's been lots of night time integration for many, many years.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Adam Clayton Powell Jr. made a speech at Manhattan Centre before the show started which put everybody in high gear.
